title: L02 · Stress & Written Accents
objective: Spanish stress is 100% predictable from spelling. Learn the two-rule system, and you can pronounce any new word correctly on first sight.
strategy: There are only TWO rules — Rule A (where stress falls by default) and Rule B (when the accent mark ´ overrides it). Drill section 1 and section 2 until they're automatic. Section 3 covers the special accents that distinguish word PAIRS (sí vs si, tú vs tu) — high-value.

sections.title: Rule A — Default stress
sections.note: If a word has NO written accent (´), stress falls in ONE of two predictable places.

blocks.text: Words ending in a vowel, -n, or -s → stress on the SECOND-TO-LAST syllable
blocks.audioText: casa, libro, hablan, comen, gracias, dificil
blocks.rule: Most Spanish words follow this. 'CA-sa', 'LI-bro', 'HA-blan'. Memorise the trigger: VOWEL / N / S = penultimate stress.

examples.translation: house / maison — stress on CA
book / livre
they speak / ils parlent
they eat / ils mangent
thanks / merci

blocks.text: Words ending in any OTHER consonant → stress on the LAST syllable
blocks.audioText: hablar, comer, vivir, hotel, papel, ciudad
blocks.rule: If the word ends in anything except a vowel, -n, or -s, then stress goes to the final syllable. Infinitives (always end -r) and many one-off nouns fall here.

examples.translation: to speak / parler
to eat / manger
to live / vivre
hotel / hôtel
city / ville

sections.title: Rule B — When you SEE an accent mark (´), it overrides Rule A
sections.note: If a word breaks Rule A, the writer signals it with a written accent on the stressed vowel. The accent ALWAYS points to where stress goes.

blocks.text: Any word with ´ → stress on the marked vowel, regardless of the ending
blocks.audioText: café, también, después, lápiz, fácil, médico
blocks.rule: Without the accent, 'cafe' would be stressed CA-fe (Rule A — ends in vowel). The accent on 'é' moves stress to the last syllable: ca-FÉ. The accent is NEVER decorative — it's a stress marker.

examples.translation: coffee, café / café — accent forces stress on é
also / aussi
after / après
pencil / crayon — without accent would be la-PIZ
easy / facile
doctor / médecin — stress on first syllable

blocks.text: Three things to NOT confuse: ´ (stress), ñ (different letter), ü (diaeresis)
blocks.rule: (1) ´ on a vowel = stress mark. (2) ~ on n = different letter (ñ). (3) ¨ on u (only after g, before e/i) = the u is pronounced (otherwise silent).

examples.translation: shame, embarrassment / honte — ¨ keeps the u sounded
penguin / pingouin
war / guerre — no ¨, so u is silent (just /g/)

sections.title: Accent marks that distinguish word PAIRS
sections.note: Spanish has ~12 word pairs where the SAME spelling is used for two meanings — distinguished only by an accent. Memorise these; they appear constantly.

blocks.audioText: Sí, hablo español. Si tengo tiempo, voy.
blocks.rule: Both pronounced the same. 'sí' (with accent) = yes. 'si' (no accent) = if.

examples.translation: Yes, I speak Spanish. / Oui, je parle espagnol.
If I have time, I'll go. / Si j'ai le temps, j'y vais.

blocks.audioText: Tú eres mi amigo. Tu casa es bonita.

examples.translation: You are my friend. / Tu es mon ami.
Your house is pretty. / Ta maison est jolie.

blocks.audioText: Él come pan. El pan es bueno.

examples.translation: He eats bread. / Il mange du pain.
The bread is good. / Le pain est bon.

blocks.audioText: Quiero más café. Lo intenté, mas no pude.
blocks.rule: 'más' (accent) = more — VERY common. 'mas' (no accent) = old-fashioned 'but' (you'll see it in literature, rarely speech).

examples.translation: I want more coffee. / Je veux plus de café.
more or less / plus ou moins — useful daily phrase

blocks.text: Question/exclamation words ALWAYS get accents: qué, quién, cómo, cuándo, dónde, cuál, cuánto, por qué
blocks.rule: When used in a question (direct or indirect) or exclamation, these words take an accent. The SAME words without an accent are conjunctions or relatives.

examples.translation: What do you want? / Qu'est-ce que tu veux?
What I want is coffee. (relative, no accent) / Ce que je veux, c'est du café.
What's your name? / Comment t'appelles-tu?
As you wish. (no accent, 'as') / Comme tu veux.
Where is the bathroom? / Où sont les toilettes?
Why are you studying Spanish? / Pourquoi étudies-tu l'espagnol?
Because I like it. (one word, no accent) / Parce que ça me plaît.

sections.title: Spanish punctuation quirks
sections.note: Two surprises for English/French speakers: opening question and exclamation marks.

blocks.rule: Spanish marks the START of a question with ¿ and the start of an exclamation with ¡. The opening mark goes EXACTLY where the question/exclamation portion begins — sometimes mid-sentence.

examples.translation: How are you? / Comment ça va?
Hi! How's it going? / Salut ! Ça va ?

examples.audioText: Si no te importa, puedes ayudarme?
examples.translation: If you don't mind, can you help me? / Si ça ne te dérange pas, peux-tu m'aider ?
examples.ipa: Notice ¿ starts mid-sentence, exactly where the question begins.
